evidence: R2 routing config contains only a default route: "prefix": "0.0.0.0", "fwd-list": [{ "fwd": "10.0.0.6" }], and there is no entry for 192.168.0.0/24 (get_running_config for R2).

R1 has a route to the destination network on R2: "prefix": "172.16.0.0", "mask": "255.255.255.0", "fwd-list": [{ "fwd": "10.0.0.2" }] (get_running_config for R1) — forward path from 192.168.0.12 → 172.16.0.12 is configured.

R1 and R2 serial link IPs match: R1 Serial0/1/0 address 10.0.0.1/30 and R2 Serial0/1/0 address 10.0.0.2/30 (get_running_config outputs), so the next-hop toward R2 is reachable/configured on both sides.

Both R1 and R2 outbound ACLs include a permissive 'permit ip any any' entry (R1: access-list R1_TO_R2 seq 20 permit ip any any; R2: access-list R2_TO_R1 seq 20 permit ip any any) — ACLs are unlikely to be blocking ICMP between the networks.

plan_history.intent_description: Source 192.168.0.12 cannot reach 172.16.0.12 because R2 lacks a return route for 192.168.0.0/24. Remediate so traffic from 172.16.0.12 is routed back to 192.168.0.12 via R1.
plan_history.problem: R2 has no route for 192.168.0.0/24; return traffic from 172.16.0.12 is forwarded to R2's default next-hop instead of R1.
plan_history.fix_summary: Add a static route on R2 for 192.168.0.0/24 with next-hop 10.0.0.1 (R1) so return traffic is sent to R1.

remaining_issues: R2 access-list R2_TO_R1 includes an explicit deny for tcp from 172.16.0.0/24 to 192.168.0.0/24 dst-port 443 (sequence 10). This may block HTTPS (TCP/443) between the subnets even though general IP traffic is permitted by sequence 20.
